name: configmaps
context: Manage ConfigMaps and Secrets in Kubernetes — externalize configuration, inject env vars, mount config files
info: ConfigMaps and Secrets externalize application configuration from container images. ConfigMaps store non-sensitive key-value data (config files, feature flags, environment variables). Secrets store sensitive data (passwords, tokens, TLS certificates) with base64 encoding and optional encryption at rest. Agents use ConfigMaps and Secrets to configure [[kubernetes/pods]] without rebuilding images — inject values as environment variables or mount them as files. Changes to ConfigMaps/Secrets can trigger Pod restarts via annotations or tools like Reloader.
triggers: mount a config file in a pod
body:
# ConfigMaps and Secrets

ConfigMaps and Secrets decouple configuration from container images. Change configuration without rebuilding or redeploying images.

## ConfigMap

### Create from YAML

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
  namespace: production
data:
  DATABASE_HOST: postgres.production.svc.cluster.local
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"
  LOG_LEVEL: info
  FEATURE_NEW_UI: "true"
  nginx.conf: |
    server {
      listen 80;
      location / {
        proxy_pass http://localhost:8080;
      }
    }
```

### Create from CLI

```bash
# From literal values
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=DATABASE_HOST=postgres.production.svc.cluster.local \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info

# From a file
kubectl create configmap nginx-config --from-file=nginx.conf

# From an env file
kubectl create configmap app-env --from-env-file=.env.production

# From a directory (each file becomes a key)
kubectl create configmap configs --from-file=./config-dir/
```

## Secrets

### Create from YAML

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
  namespace: production
type: Opaque
stringData:       # plain text — Kubernetes encodes to base64
  username: admin
  password: s3cret-p@ssw0rd
  connection-string: postgresql://admin:s3cret-p@ssw0rd@postgres:5432/mydb
```

### Create from CLI

```bash
# From literal values
kubectl create secret generic db-credentials \
  --from-literal=username=admin \
  --from-literal=password='s3cret-p@ssw0rd'

# TLS secret from cert and key files
kubectl create secret tls my-tls-secret \
  --cert=./tls.crt \
  --key=./tls.key

# Docker registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=myregistry.io \
  --docker-username=user \
  --docker-password=token

# View decoded secret value
kubectl get secret db-credentials -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
```

## Consuming in Pods

### As Environment Variables

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: myregistry/my-app:v1.2.0
      env:
        # Single key from ConfigMap
        - name: LOG_LEVEL
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: app-config
              key: LOG_LEVEL
        # Single key from Secret
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: password
      # All keys from ConfigMap as env vars
      envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: app-config
        - secretRef:
            name: db-credentials
```

### As Mounted Files

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: myregistry/my-app:v1.2.0
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/app/config
          readOnly: true
        - name: tls-volume
          mountPath: /etc/tls
          readOnly: true
  volumes:
    - name: config-volume
      configMap:
        name: app-config
        items:                  # optional: select specific keys
          - key: nginx.conf
            path: nginx.conf   # file name inside mount path
    - name: tls-volume
      secret:
        secretName: my-tls-secret
```

Mounted ConfigMaps are automatically updated when the ConfigMap changes (within kubelet sync period, ~1 minute). Env vars are NOT updated — they require a Pod restart.

## kubectl Commands

```bash
# List
kubectl get configmaps -n production
kubectl get secrets -n production

# Describe
kubectl describe configmap app-config
kubectl describe secret db-credentials

# View data
kubectl get configmap app-config -o yaml
kubectl get secret db-credentials -o yaml  # base64 encoded

# Edit
kubectl edit configmap app-config

# Delete
kubectl delete configmap app-config
kubectl delete secret db-credentials
```

## Secret Types

| Type | Purpose |
|------|----------|
| `Opaque` | Arbitrary key-value data (default) |
| `kubernetes.io/tls` | TLS certificate and key |
| `kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson` | Docker registry credentials |
| `kubernetes.io/basic-auth` | Username and password |
| `kubernetes.io/ssh-auth` | SSH private key |
| `kubernetes.io/service-account-token` | ServiceAccount token (auto-created) |

## Security Considerations

- Secrets are base64-encoded, **not encrypted** by default
- Enable encryption at rest in the API server (`EncryptionConfiguration`)
- Use RBAC to restrict Secret access: [[kubernetes/rbac]]
- Consider external secret managers: AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, External Secrets Operator
- Never commit Secrets to git — use sealed-secrets or external-secrets

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace |

sections.title: ConfigMaps
sections.body: ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs. Create from literals, files, directories, or env files. Keys become env var names or file names when consumed by Pods. Mounted ConfigMaps auto-update when changed (file mounts only — env vars require Pod restart). Maximum size: 1 MiB per ConfigMap.

sections.title: Secrets
sections.body: Secrets store sensitive data (passwords, tokens, certificates) with base64 encoding. Types: Opaque (generic), TLS (cert+key), docker-registry (image pull), basic-auth, ssh-auth. Secrets are stored in etcd — enable encryption at rest for production. Use [[kubernetes/rbac]] to restrict access. For external secret management, use External Secrets Operator with AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or GCP Secret Manager.

sections.title: Consuming config in Pods
sections.body: Two methods: 1) **Environment variables** — `valueFrom.configMapKeyRef` or `secretKeyRef` for single keys, `envFrom` for all keys. Simple but not auto-updated. 2) **Volume mounts** — mount as files at a path. Auto-updated when source changes. Use `items` to select specific keys. Use `subPath` to mount a single file without replacing the directory. Mounted Secrets have file permissions 0644 by default — set `defaultMode: 0400` for restrictive permissions.

links.context: Pods consume ConfigMaps and Secrets as env vars or file mounts
Update ConfigMaps to change Deployment configuration
ConfigMaps and Secrets can be mounted as volumes
RBAC controls who can read Secrets

related.note: Pods are the consumers of ConfigMaps and Secrets
RBAC restricts who can access Secrets
ConfigMaps and Secrets are projected as volume types

scripts.name: Create ConfigMap
scripts.description: Create a ConfigMap from literal key-value pairs
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
NAME=${1:?Usage: create-configmap.sh <name> <key=value> [key=value...]}
shift
NS=${KUBE_NAMESPACE:-default}

ARGS=()
for KV in "$@"; do
  ARGS+=("--from-literal=$KV")
done

kubectl create configmap "$NAME" "${ARGS[@]}" -n "$NS" --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
echo "ConfigMap $NAME created/updated in namespace $NS"
kubectl get configmap "$NAME" -n "$NS" -o yaml

scripts.name: Create Secret
scripts.description: Create a generic Secret from literal key-value pairs
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
NAME=${1:?Usage: create-secret.sh <name> <key=value> [key=value...]}
shift
NS=${KUBE_NAMESPACE:-default}

ARGS=()
for KV in "$@"; do
  ARGS+=("--from-literal=$KV")
done

kubectl create secret generic "$NAME" "${ARGS[@]}" -n "$NS" --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
echo "Secret $NAME created/updated in namespace $NS"

isSecret.description: Whether to create a Secret instead of ConfigMap
